past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 45, managed with metformin
Dental abscess with extraction of lower right molar 6 weeks ago, treated with amoxicillin-clavulanate
Remote history of optic neuritis in the left eye at age 31, evaluated for multiple sclerosis at the time with inconclusive MRI findings, no further demyelinating events since
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 68, was a heavy smoker
Mother alive with Alzheimer's disease diagnosed at age 74
One sister, age 44, diagnosed with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis at age 38
Paternal uncle with glioblastoma, died at age 55
No family history of seizure disorders

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 beers per week, none in the past 2 weeks due to feeling unwell
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 10 years ago, approximately 5 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in a suburban home
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 3 miles daily, has not exercised in 3 weeks due to headaches and fatigue

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and date. Mildly inattentive, requiring repetition of digit span (5 forward, 3 backward, low normal). Speech is fluent but with occasional word-finding pauses. Naming 13/15 on Boston Naming Test sample items with two semantic paraphasias. Repetition intact. Comprehension intact for simple commands, mild difficulty with two-step commands. Registration 3/3, recall 2/3 at 5 minutes. Clock drawing mildly abnormal with misplacement of numbers on the left side of the clock face.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation but patient reports a vague sense that objects on the left are less vivid. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout except for subtle left pronator drift noted on sustained arm extension over 30 seconds. No obvious weakness on direct testing. No tremor or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Mild left-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation of the hands, not consistently reproducible.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally. Left Achilles 3+, right Achilles 2+. Left plantar response equivocal, right plantar flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements slightly slower on the left, though patient is right-handed.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based but steady. Tandem gait mildly unsteady with one misstep. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a brief episode lasting approximately 15 seconds in which he paused, stared blankly, and his left hand exhibited rhythmic twitching. He was briefly confused afterward for approximately 30 seconds and did not recall the event. This was witnessed by the examining physician.

patient.chief_complaint: New-onset seizure and worsening headaches for 3 weeks, with recent dental infection raising concern for brain abscess
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Kowalski is a 48-year-old right-handed high school history teacher who presents to the emergency department after experiencing his first witnessed seizure at home this morning. His wife reports that while eating breakfast, he suddenly stopped talking, stared vacantly for several seconds, and then his left arm began shaking rhythmically. The episode lasted approximately 30 seconds and was followed by confusion lasting about 5 minutes. He has no recollection of the event.

For the past three weeks, he has experienced progressive headaches that are predominantly right-sided, worse in the morning upon waking, and partially responsive to ibuprofen. Over the past week, the headaches have become more severe and are now accompanied by nausea, particularly when bending over or straining. His wife has noticed that he has become increasingly forgetful and has had difficulty finding words during conversations, which she initially attributed to stress at work. Two days ago, he had an episode at school where he lost his train of thought mid-lecture and stood silently at the podium for about 10 seconds before resuming, which concerned his students enough that the school called his wife.

Of particular concern is that six weeks ago he underwent extraction of a lower right molar complicated by a dental abscess. He was treated with a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for 10 days. His dentist cleared him at a follow-up visit two weeks later, noting the socket was healing appropriately. However, the emergency physician is concerned that the dental infection may have seeded hematogenously to the brain, particularly given the ring-enhancing lesion seen on the initial CT scan.

Adding to the diagnostic uncertainty is his history of a single episode of optic neuritis at age 31. He was evaluated for multiple sclerosis at the time with brain MRI that showed two small non-specific white matter lesions. He was followed by neurology for two years without further events and was ultimately not diagnosed with MS. His sister was subsequently diagnosed with relapsing-remitting MS six years ago. The emergency department team is now questioning whether this could represent a late presentation of tumefactive multiple sclerosis rather than a tumor or abscess.

He denies any recent fevers, chills, night sweats, or weight loss. He has not traveled recently and has no exposure to tuberculosis. He denies any history of cancer, though his father died of lung cancer and his paternal uncle died of a brain tumor.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8-9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric but slightly slower than expected for age, reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Mild right hemisphere amplitude attenuation compared to left

findings.frequency: Intermittent polymorphic delta activity at 1-2 Hz
Intermittent bursts of 3-4 Hz theta activity lasting 2-5 seconds
Rhythmic alpha-theta activity at 7-8 Hz evolving to 3-4 Hz over 18 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp-and-slow wave complexes with evolution in frequency and spatial distribution

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to concern for elevated intracranial pressure
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving at mid-frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating right frontotemporal focal slowing and one captured electrographic focal seizure arising from the right frontotemporal region. Additionally, intermittent generalized slowing is present, which may reflect elevated intracranial pressure, metabolic factors, or diffuse cerebral dysfunction. The generalized slowing partially obscures the focal nature of findings and could be misleading regarding lateralization. Clinical and imaging correlation is essential. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: No sleep captured. Muscle artifact intermittently obscured temporal regions. The generalized slowing complicates interpretation of focal features.

findings.location: Right frontotemporal region, centered at the junction of the right frontal and temporal lobes, involving the insula

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense center with isointense rim
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous with hyperintense center and surrounding edema
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Bright ring with extensive perilesional FLAIR hyperintensity extending through the right temporal lobe and into the frontal white matter
signal_characteristics.DWI: Equivocal restricted diffusion — patchy areas of restricted diffusion at the rim but NOT the classic homogeneous central restriction seen in abscess. Some areas of restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim consistent with high cellularity.
signal_characteristics.ADC: Mixed ADC values centrally; some areas of low ADC at rim, higher ADC centrally but not uniformly so
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular components along the medial wall. Enhancement is heterogeneous and somewhat shaggy rather than smooth.

findings.mass_effect: Moderate mass effect with compression of the right lateral ventricle and 4 mm leftward midline shift at the level of the septum pellucidum
findings.borders: Irregular, with finger-like projections of FLAIR signal extending along white matter tracts suggesting infiltrative process

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, adjacent to the left lateral ventricle body
findings.borders: Ovoid, well-defined, perpendicular to the ventricular surface

additional_observations: Two small non-enhancing white matter lesions (left periventricular and right juxtacortical) raise the possibility of demyelinating disease, though their morphology is non-specific
The enhancing rim of the dominant lesion is thick and irregular, more suggestive of neoplasm than abscess, though abscess cannot be excluded
The perilesional FLAIR signal is extensive, disproportionate to mass size, which could indicate either vasogenic edema from tumor or inflammatory edema from abscess or demyelination
No additional enhancing lesions to suggest metastatic disease
mri.impression: Large right frontotemporal ring-enhancing lesion with irregular, thick enhancing rim, central necrosis or liquefaction, moderate mass effect, and extensive surrounding edema. However, the differential is broadened by: (1) the recent dental abscess raising concern for hematogenous brain abscess, (2) the equivocal diffusion characteristics that do not clearly distinguish abscess from tumor, and (3) the presence of two small non-enhancing white matter lesions that, combined with the patient's history of optic neuritis and family history of MS, raise the less likely possibility of tumefactive demyelination. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 148 mEq/L (H), Glucose 171 mg/dL (H), CRP 9.0 mg/L (H), ESR 27 mm/hr (H), HbA1c 7.8 % (H).

findings.signal: rCBV_enhancing_rim: Markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 3.8 relative to contralateral normal white matter) in the enhancing rim, particularly along the lateral and posterior margins; rCBV_central: Low rCBV centrally consistent with necrosis; rCBV_perilesional: Mildly elevated rCBV in perilesional tissue extending beyond the enhancing margin, suggesting infiltrative tumor; perfusion_pattern: Hyperperfusion of the enhancing rim with central hypoperfusion — pattern highly suggestive of high-grade neoplasm rather than abscess or demyelination

output.impression: The finding of mildly elevated rCBV in the perilesional region suggests tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Right frontotemporal lesion, voxels placed at enhancing rim and perilesional tissue
findings.signal: choline: Markedly elevated choline peak at the enhancing rim (Cho/Cr ratio 3.2, normal <1.5); NAA: Markedly reduced N-acetylaspartate (NAA/Cr ratio 0.4, normal >1.5), indicating severe neuronal loss or displacement; lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks centrally, consistent with necrosis; amino_acids: No prominent amino acid peaks (leucine, valine, isoleucine) that would suggest abscess; myoinositol: Mildly elevated myoinositol in perilesional tissue; Cho_NAA_ratio: 8.0 at enhancing rim (highly suggestive of high-grade neoplasm, threshold >2.0 suspicious)

output.impression: The absence of amino acid peaks argues strongly against brain abscess. Combined with the perfusion findings, the imaging diagnosis of glioblastoma (WHO grade 4) is strongly favored. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Normal size and attenuation, no focal lesions

Histopathology.value: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis

Molecular Profile.value: Present (gain of chromosome 7, loss of chromosome 10)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Morphology High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis  (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 35%  (H), IDH1/IDH2 mutation Wildtype (negative)  (H), MGMT promoter methylation Unmethylated  (H), TERT promoter mutation Present  (H), EGFR amplification Present  (H), +7/-10 chromosomal changes Present (gain of chromosome 7, loss of chromosome 10)  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Morphology: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis (H)
+7/-10 chromosomal changes: Present (gain of chromosome 7, loss of chromosome 10) (H)

findings.signal: Broca_area: Robust activation in left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca's area), well lateralized to the left hemisphere; Wernicke_area: Activation in left posterior superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke's area); laterality_index: Language laterality index 0.82 (strongly left-lateralized); proximity_to_lesion: The right frontotemporal lesion is contralateral to the dominant language network. Minimal risk to language function from right-sided resection.

left_hand_motor: Activation in right precentral gyrus for left hand motor tasks. The inferior-lateral aspect of the right precentral gyrus activation is within 12 mm of the superior margin of the enhancing lesion.; right_hand_motor: Normal activation in left precentral gyrus; supplementary_motor_area: Bilateral SMA activation, slightly right-lateralized

output.impression: However, the right primary motor cortex for left upper extremity function is in close proximity (12 mm) to the superior tumor margin, and DTI shows displacement of the right corticospinal tract. Clinical correlation recommended.

Demyelination Markers.value: Mildly prolonged P100 latency on the left (118 ms), normal on the right (102 ms)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Visual evoked potentials Mildly prolonged P100 latency on the left (118 ms), normal on the right (102 ms) ms (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Visual evoked potentials: Mildly prolonged P100 latency on the left (118 ms), normal on the right (102 ms) ms (H)

differential.likelihood: Moderate initially, reduced after perfusion/spectroscopy and negative infectious workup
differential.key_features: Recent dental abscess provides plausible infectious source; ring-enhancing lesion on MRI; however, thick irregular rim, elevated rCBV, elevated choline on spectroscopy, and absence of amino acid peaks all argue against abscess

differential.likelihood: Low-moderate initially, reduced after perfusion and demyelination workup
differential.key_features: History of optic neuritis, family history of MS, small white matter lesions on MRI; however, the high rCBV, spectroscopy pattern, thick irregular enhancement, and negative MOG/NMO antibodies argue against demyelination

Solitary ring-enhancing lesion could represent metastasis; father with lung cancer raises concern; however, negative CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, no known primary, and infiltrative borders on MRI favor primary brain tumor
Ring enhancement is atypical for CNS lymphoma (usually homogeneous); patient is immunocompetent; spectroscopy and perfusion patterns not typical for lymphoma

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiseizure medication (levetiracetam) given witnessed focal seizure with impaired awareness
Administer dexamethasone for vasogenic edema and mass effect
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in perilesional edema and symptom improvement

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with contrast, perfusion, and spectroscopy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Ring-enhancing lesion with elevated rCBV and elevated choline, favoring high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Obtain blood cultures and inflammatory markers to evaluate for infectious etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative blood cultures and low procalcitonin arguing against active infection

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest, abdomen, pelvis to rule out primary malignancy and metastatic disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative metastatic workup supporting primary brain tumor

optimal_actions.action: Consider and evaluate for tumefactive demyelination given patient's history of optic neuritis and family history of MS
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative NMO and MOG antibodies; prolonged left VEP consistent with old optic neuritis but not active MS

optimal_actions.action: Obtain neurosurgical consultation for stereotactic biopsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Tissue diagnosis confirming glioblastoma IDH-wildtype with molecular profiling

optimal_actions.action: Order functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left-lateralized language with motor cortex proximity requiring careful surgical planning

optimal_actions.action: Plan maximal safe surgical resection with intraoperative neuromonitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Gross total or near-total resection while preserving motor function

optimal_actions.action: Initiate Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation) followed by adjuvant temozolomide
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard of care treatment initiated, with counseling about reduced temozolomide benefit given unmethylated MGMT

critical_actions: Initiate antiseizure medication after witnessed seizure
Administer corticosteroids (dexamethasone) for cerebral edema with mass effect
Obtain advanced neuroimaging (perfusion and spectroscopy) to differentiate glioma from abscess and demyelination
Obtain tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection before definitive treatment
Rule out brain abscess given recent dental infection before committing to a purely oncologic treatment plan
Obtain molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) to guide treatment decisions
contraindicated_actions: Empiric brain abscess aspiration without advanced imaging to differentiate tumor from abscess, as this could cause hemorrhage in a highly vascular tumor
Lumbar puncture in the setting of a large mass lesion with midline shift, due to risk of cerebral herniation
Administering high-dose steroids before considering that steroid response could falsely support a diagnosis of lymphoma or tumefactive MS and delay appropriate biopsy
Delaying antiseizure medication after witnessed seizure in a patient with a brain mass
Starting empiric antibiotics for brain abscess without obtaining tissue diagnosis, potentially delaying cancer treatment
key_reasoning_points: The recent dental abscess creates a plausible but ultimately incorrect narrative for brain abscess — the clinician must not anchor on this red herring despite its temporal association
The patient's history of optic neuritis and family history of MS create a second red herring suggesting tumefactive demyelination — advanced imaging and serologic testing effectively exclude this

The ring-enhancing pattern on initial MRI is non-specific and shared by glioma, abscess, and tumefactive MS — advanced MRI techniques (perfusion showing elevated rCBV, spectroscopy showing elevated choline without amino acid peaks) are essential to narrow the differential

DWI findings in this case are intentionally equivocal — classic teaching that restricted diffusion centrally equals abscess is an oversimplification, as high-grade gliomas can show restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim due to high cellularity

Normal WBC, negative blood cultures, and normal procalcitonin collectively argue against active infection, but the clinician must recognize that a walled-off abscess can present with normal peripheral markers
The two small white matter lesions are incidental distractors that add weight to the MS red herring but do not meet diagnostic criteria for MS
Tissue diagnosis is ultimately required regardless of imaging findings — empiric treatment for any of the differential diagnoses without histopathological confirmation would be inappropriate

metadata.clinical_complexity_notes: Diagnostic puzzle featuring three competing diagnoses (glioblastoma, brain abscess, tumefactive MS) with red herrings from recent dental infection and prior optic neuritis. Requires advanced neuroimaging and systematic exclusion of mimics.
educational_objectives: Differentiate ring-enhancing brain lesions using advanced MRI techniques
Recognize limitations of conventional MRI diffusion-weighted imaging in distinguishing tumor from abscess
Understand the role of MR perfusion (rCBV) and spectroscopy in brain tumor characterization
Avoid anchoring bias from compelling but ultimately misleading clinical history
Emphasize the necessity of tissue diagnosis in ambiguous intracranial mass lesions
